Commander Vance stared into the vast galactic abyss as the starship's primary propulsion engines went silent. He had been worried about his command, and even more of what it meant for Captain Stacker Mire's ship.

But now, no one was looking. None but the pilots and scientists who had flown the ship would ever know exactly what had happened. The silence meant the ship had done its job. The only one who might ever find out was the one who had to live with the consequences; Captain Stacker Mire.

Vance had a good reason to be worried, though. Stacker Mire's ship, Starhawk, had disappeared.

It had been less than three weeks since the ship had returned to Earth after a long journey that took it to the distant planet of T'Soni. The gothic estate held secrets that the villagers spoke of only in hushed, terrified whispers after dusk. The man who lived there was known as Lord Vork. The servants and slaves who worked the property were considered second-class citizens and often treated with little respect by the residents. The servants' jobs, along with the few meals that they could afford, often fell to their young charges. The children of these servants and slaves were often kidnapped by the gothic people, who would take their parents and leave them with the estate, where they would work for weeks or months (or even years) before being returned to their parents or taken and sold by the gothic people. There were only the occasional reports of the gothic people enslaving outsiders such as humans. Although it seems unlikely, the majority of the local population was not completely evil, and was often seen running errands around the mansion and its surrounding acreage as well as tending the grounds.

The world was already extinguished in darkness.

Dr. Aris discovered a mysterious biological anomaly buried deep beneath the Antarctic ice sheet that defied evolution. Its discovery also unleashed an ancient power into an unsuspecting human world. The world's governments were unprepared for the threat that this strange anomaly represented, and the most advanced science of the time came up empty.

In the years since, Dr. Aris has dedicated himself to finding the answers to this mystery. Through time and space he has been joined by other human explorers, including Aris's own daughter, Amelia. But in recent years the Earth has also come under attack from mysterious aliens. The situation seems hopeless, but the mysterious aliens hold one thing -- the ultimate weapon.

With only a single surviving expeditionary group, Dr. Aris leads a new expedition to the Antarctic. This time he finds a far more dangerous threat.

The alchemist carefully combined the volatile elements, hoping to unlock the secret to eternal vitality. With this knowledge, he infused his own body with that of a dragon, and transformed it into a living being of pure element. He then cast out of his body and into the elemental plane of air.

The alchemist then travelled east to an unknown land, where he remained for many years in the depths of the mountain where he had created it, waiting for the perfect recipe for life and immortality to be found.

At last, all his labour was rewarded and his soul became fully restored for him. Thus, the alchemist returned home, but the quest for life had not yet ended.

The alchemist's soul rested within a small cave off the mountain road. Every day, he would return to his cave, gathering the elements needed to create the final recipe. If the soul had any unfinished business, it would then go out and meet with another soul who was also searching for immortality. This cycle repeated until the soul found the perfect recipe to bring him eternal life.

The colony even began to use a satellite to track the pulsars in order to determine if they were producing pulsing signals—which would be evidence of intelligent life. She turned. Her dark hair was a tangled mass of red and black. Her skin was pale, the skin of an emaciated ghoul. The woman's eyes were green, and they stared directly at him.


Her dark lips were stretched thin and sharp. Her eyes were cold and seething. A terrible smile touched the detective's lips as he recognized the glimmering green eyes of his wife. He tried to ignore the growing stench emanating from her.


She stepped forward, her long, slim arms extended towards the detective and he could hear her exhale in the stale air of the cemetery. Her hands trembled even in the dark. She was trembling so violently his fingers left bloody tracks in the dirt.

But even though trade-in programs are credited with raising new vehicle sales, it's hard for manufacturers to measure how effective they are. Most automakers don't keep track of the sales data that's collected through the program.

"There are just too many factors that go into the decision to buy a car or pay cash for something," says Michael Green, vice president of vehicle research at J.D. Power & Associates. These dealership markups on hybrid models are getting completely out of hand nowadays, it's annoying.

The price of a Ford Fusion Hybrid is not exactly the same as the price on a comparable petrol version of the same model.

This price hike, according to the Carat, will see the average price rise by 40 Euros per month, or 50% over the three-year period ending on June 30.

According to a Volkswagen spokesman, the new cost of a hybrid car is not yet available, but he added that "we can foresee a rise, due to the increasing number of hybrids on our roads."

In comparison, the average cost of a non-hybrid car on the European market is only 15 Euros more per month.

This means the average new hybrid buyer will be paying 80% more than the average new petrol buyer.

Hybrids represent just 7.5% of sales in the UK, and the average price of a hybrid car that's been bought new will only be 20% more than the value of the vehicle itself.

We are going to start reducing unemployment by offering up a very generous unemployment check.

Honestly, reading through political commentary on social media just puts me in a terrible mood. So many times the comments are not worth the time to read through for context (unless you're a troll) and it often can distract as a commenter is usually making a point they feel they are not getting a fair hearing. And if the argument comes down to an issue one side feels strongly about, it is hard to see how they could be convinced that their position would be supported by a reasonable person with an open mind. My own take on this is I am not sure my feelings have changed at any time, regardless of our arguments or the content.

To put it another way, when one side is so wrong and makes so many accusations we often need an extra layer of context to understand.

Political debates nowadays just feel like a giant shouting match instead of actual discussions about real policies. In a previous interview I had with Tim Lachmann (@Tim_Lachmann) about our book, Heresies & Rifts, he said that he found this phenomenon "disheartening." It's a sentiment that I echoed at the time and it appears that many more contemporary libertarians do as well.

In this book, the three authors make the case for why the state is needed and what the alternative is. The problem with many of today's libertarians is a lack of understanding about the issues, which is largely due to the influence of an ideological movement that has hijacked the libertarian movement. If today's libertarians don't start acting as libertarian intellectuals and start learning from past libertarians from the classical and modern periods, we may be on the way to being a movement which has no real connection to libertarian values and history.

The latest jobs report came out today, and it looks like the employment numbers are finally stabilizing a bit. The U-6 unemployment rate had fallen to 9.5% for June, the lowest level since May 2015. (This is still very high by historical standards, and certainly much higher than at any point since the recession of the late 1980s.) Still, despite all the progress the US economy has made, not everyone is feeling the good news. The unemployment rate hit an 11-year high and the labor force participation rate is at 62.6%, down from 63.8% in May. Not everyone who wants a work is going to be able to find one. For example, the percentage of people working part-time for economic reasons is up to 4.1%, which is more than double the level the employment recovery can sustain.
